scene.description: Engine 181 has a 250' 1¾" line stretched to a second-floor apartment fire. The fog nozzle is flowing 185 GPM at 50 PSI. Add 5 PSI for the floor above the pump. No appliance loss. What pump discharge pressure should you set?

problems.title: Extended 1¾ Line to Second Floor
problems.question: Engine 181 has a 250 ft 1¾-inch line stretched to a second-floor apartment fire. The fog nozzle is flowing 185 GPM at 50 PSI. The nozzle is on the second floor; use height = (floor number - 1) × 10 ft and elevation pressure = height × 0.5 PSI/ft. No appliance loss. Use C = 15.5. What pump discharge pressure should you set?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds of feet.
problems.instructorExplanation: This variation uses 250 ft of 1¾-inch hose, 185 GPM, 5 PSI elevation, and 0 PSI appliance loss. Add those values after calculating friction loss.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use the same answer for every similar-looking single-line problem. Check length, flow, elevation, and appliance loss in the question.

formulaBreakdown: FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds of feet.

problems.title: Extended 1¾ Line to Second Floor
problems.question: Engine 181 has a 250 ft 1¾-inch line stretched to a second-floor apartment fire. The fog nozzle is flowing 150 GPM at 50 PSI. The nozzle is on the second floor; use height = (floor number - 1) × 10 ft and elevation pressure = height × 0.5 PSI/ft. No appliance loss. Use C = 15.5. What pump discharge pressure should you set?

problems.title: Extended 1¾ Line to Second Floor
problems.question: Engine 181 has a 250 ft 1¾-inch line stretched to a second-floor apartment fire. The fog nozzle is flowing 185 GPM at 50 PSI. The nozzle is on the second floor; use height = (floor number - 1) × 10 ft and elevation pressure = height × 0.5 PSI/ft. Add 10 PSI appliance loss for the appliance. Use C = 15.5. What pump discharge pressure should you set?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds of feet.
problems.instructorExplanation: This variation uses 250 ft of 1¾-inch hose, 185 GPM, 5 PSI elevation, and 10 PSI appliance loss. Add those values after calculating friction loss.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use the same answer for every similar-looking single-line problem. Check length, flow, elevation, and appliance loss in the question.
